---
title: "Toward Precision Continuing Care: Predicting Lapse Risk in Substance Use Disorders Using Machine Learning"
author: "Kendra Wyant"
date: "April 9, 2026"
institute: "University of Wisconsin-Madison"
format: 
  revealjs:
    css: slides.css
    incremental: false  
    auto-stretch: false
    logo: images/arc_logo.png
    footer: "Dissertation Defense"
slide-number: false
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: images/title_slide.png
  data-background-size: cover
  data-background-repeat: no
  data-background-position: right top
---



## Substance Use Disorders

::: {.columns}

:::{.column width = "66%"}

- Chronic conditions

:::{.fragment}
- Initial treatments are effective

:::{}
- 1 in 10 individuals receive treatment
:::
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Episodic approach to care
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Treatment disparities exist
:::


:::

:::{.column width = "4%"}
:::

:::{.column width = "30%"}

![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/crowd.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 right=0 height=80%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Substance use disorders are chronic conditions with high relapse rates, substantial co-morbidity with mental and physical health issues, and increased mortality risk.

We have effective Medication and psychosocial interventions. But, nearly 90% of people with a substance use disorder do not receive any form of treatment. 

For those who do receive care, treatment is often brief and episodic. Many individuals enter care only when symptoms are severe and risk is high. At the same time, services are time-limited, resources are constrained, and providers frequently operate at or beyond capacity. As a result, patients often leave treatment without adequate supports in place, contributing to a “revolving door” pattern in which individuals cycle repeatedly through acute episodes of care.

These limitations are magnified for people from certain demographic subgroups who may face unique barriers to treatment, including financial constraints, limited service availability, and mobility or transportation challenges. And marginalized groups may additionally experience minority stress, stigma, discrimination, and isolation leading to higher rates of substance use disorders and poorer treatment outcomes.

:::


##


![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/diabetes_2.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 left=200 height="80%"}



:::{.notes}
Our treatment for substance use disorders stands in stark contrast to how we treat other chronic conditions.

When someone has diabetes, we would never expect a brief episode of care to be sufficient; instead, we rely on ongoing monitoring, adherence to treatment, regular clinical follow-up, and healthy lifestyle choices to manage risk over time. 

Substance use disorders are no different—except in how we treat them.
:::

## Continuing Care


- Continuing care is gold-standard for chronic conditions


:::{.fragment}
- Current SUD treatment infrastructure lacks capacity for long-term clinician-delivered care
:::

::: {.fragment}
- Individuals are left to navigate recovery alone
:::

:::{.notes}
Continuing care is the gold standard for chronic health conditions. Similarly, substance use treatment is most effective when provided over longer durations and with active efforts to keep patients engaged in their recovery. 

But our current system simply does not have the capacity to deliver long‑term, clinician‑led care at scale.

Without ongoing support, people are left to navigate recovery on their own, asking:
How risky is today? What do I need right now? Which recovery tool should I use and when?
:::


## Relapse Prevention

::: {.columns}

:::{.column width = "66%"}

- Recovery is a non-linear process

:::{.fragment}
- Lapses serve as early warning signs of relapse
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Risk factors differ across individuals and within individuals over time
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Self-monitoring is difficult
:::


:::

:::{.column width = "4%"}
:::

:::{.column width = "30%"}

![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/wave.jpg?raw=true)
:::
:::



:::{.notes}
The challenge is recovery is not linear. 

Lapses, or instances of goal‑inconsistent substance use, are common and and can occur at any point during recovery. On their own, they do not constitute relapse. But they must precede relapse, making them meaningful early warning signs.

The relapse prevention literature has identified a wide range of precipitants of lapse. But these risk factors fluctuate from day-to-day and differ across individuals and within individuals over time. 

They interact with one another and with interpersonal and environmental situations to influence lapse risk.

Given the complex nature of lapse risk, self-monitoring is difficult.
:::


## Recovery Monitoring and Support System

::: {.columns}
:::{.column width = "50%"}

:::{.fragment}
- Personalized adaptive recommendations
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Prompt individuals to engage with support at times of high risk
:::


:::{.fragment}
- Scalable to address unmet need
:::
:::

:::{.column width = "50%"}
![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/sensingsystem2.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 right=0 height=80%}
:::
:::

:::{.notes}

An algorithm-guided recovery monitoring and support system could help individuals monitor their current and future risk of lapse after initial treatment and adjust their lifestyle, behaviors and support accordingly.

Such a system could offer personalized, adaptive recommendations aligned with evidenced-based care and prompt individuals to engage with support at times of high risk. 

For example, individuals could receive daily messages about changes in their lapse risk, along with recommendations based on their key risk factors, like an urge-surfing recommendation for someone experiencing strong cravings.

Moreover, it would provide a scalable option for long-term monitoring, addressing the substantial unmet need for continuing care in substance use disorder treatment.

:::


## Recovery Monitoring and Support System

::: {.columns}
:::{.column width = "49%"}

1. Collect densely sampled risk-relevant data

:::{.fragment}
2. Access an accurate and interpretable model
:::

:::{.fragment}
3. Perform fairly
:::

:::

:::{.column width = "1%"}
:::

:::{.column width = "50%"}
![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/sensingsystem2.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 right=0 height=80%}
:::

:::

:::{.notes}

For such a system to exist, we need at least three things. 
One, the system must be able to collect a rich and densely sampled source (or sources) of risk-relevant data.

Two, the system must have access to a model that can predict substance use with high performance and have interpretable model inputs for support recommendations to be mapped onto. 

Three, the model must perform fairly. Algorithms that perform favorably for only a single majority group could widen existing disparities in access to resources and important clinical outcomes
:::



## Sensing Data and Machine Learning


- 91% of Americans own a smartphone 

:::{}
- At least 85% ownership across across gender, race/ethnicity, socioeconomic status, and geographic location subgroups
:::

::: {.fragment}
- Frequent longitudinal measurement of proximal risk
:::

:::{.fragment}
- An array of techniques from machine learning to predict lapse risk and highlight prospective risk factors
:::

:::{.notes}

Advances in both smartphone sensing and machine learning now make this possible. 

The proliferation of smartphone usage in recent years makes smartphone sensing a potentially scalable and equitable method for improving lapse risk monitoring. As of 2025, roughly 91% of Americans own a smartphone and smartphone ownership is consistently high across important demographic subgroups.

Smartphone sensing approaches can provide the frequent, longitudinal measurement of proximal risk factors that is necessary for prediction of future lapses with high temporal precision.


Machine learning models can handle high dimensional sensing feature sets and can accommodate non-linear and interactive relationships between features and lapse probability. And methods from interpretable machine learning can be used to understand which risk features contribute most strongly to a lapse prediction for a specific individual at a specific moment in time. 

Two sensing methods I am particularly excited about are EMA and geolocation sensing.
:::




## Ecological Momentary Assessment

:::{.columns}
:::{.column width = "58%"}
- Direct and frequent insight into subjective feelings and experiences

:::{.fragment}
- Constructs easily map onto well-studied risk factors for lapse
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Appears to be well-tolerated
:::

:::

:::{.column width = "2%"}
:::


:::{.column width = "40%"}
![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/ema.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 right=0 height=80%}
:::
:::

:::{.notes}
Ecological momentary assessment, or EMA, consists of daily or more frequent brief surveys optimized for someone to complete on their smartphone. This signal is unique in that it provides privileged access into subjective momentary experiences and allows for measuring several face-valid constructs of the relapse prevention model 

e.g., we can ask people to directly report their craving, their level of self-efficacy, and their affect. 


EMA appears to be well tolerated with adherence rates in substance using populations being comparable to other clinical samples.

:::


## Geolocation 

:::{.columns}
:::{.column width = "55%"}

- Passively sensed

:::{.fragment}
- May provide insight into information difficult to measure with self-report
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Locations can be contextualized
:::


:::

:::{.column width = "5%"}
:::


:::{.column width = "40%"}
![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/location.jpg?raw=true){.absolute top=100 right=0 height=80%}
:::
:::

:::{.notes}
Geolocation sensing is a passive sensing method that could complement EMA well. It may provide a distinct set of intervenable features from information that is difficult to measure with self-report or that would add additional burden by increasing the number of questions on daily surveys.

e.g., low location variance or increased time spent at home could signal reduced mobility, social withdrawal, or disengagement

geolocation features may become even more powerful when combined with contextual information from public sources (e.g., census data, alcohol outlet density data) or from self-report (e.g., self-evaluated riskiness of specific locations). 

:::


## Recovery Monitoring and Support System

::: {.columns}
:::{.column width = "49%"}

1. Collect densely sampled risk-relevant data

:::{}
2. Access to an accurate and interpretable model
:::

:::{}
3. Perform fairly
:::

:::

:::{.column width = "1%"}
:::

:::{.column width = "50%"}
![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/sensingsystem2.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 right=0 height=80%}
:::

:::

:::{.notes}
Today I am going to present a series of studies aimed at improving continuing care for SUDS by demonstrating the feasibility of building an automated recovery monitoring and support system. Across three studies, I will address the key features needed for such a system, including assessing potential sensing methods for their ability to predict lapse accurately and timely and evaluating the interpretability and fairness of model outputs.

:::


## Specific Aims

1. Evaluate and interpret models that predict <span style="color: #c5050c;">**immediate risk**</span> of a lapse back to alcohol use using 4x daily EMA

2. Evaluate and interpret models that predict <span style="color: #c5050c;">**future risk**</span> of a lapse back to alcohol use using 4x daily EMA

3. Evaluate and interpret models that predict <span style="color: #c5050c;">**immediate risk**</span> of a lapse back to opioid use using 1x daily EMA and geolocation sensing


:::{.notes}
To achieve this goal, we used two clinical samples of participants in early recovery from alcohol use disorder (Studies 1–2) and opioid use disorder (Study 3) with reported goals of abstinence. 

In Study 1, we evaluated and interpreted three separate models that provided probabilities of an immediate lapse back to alcohol use with increasing temporal precision: lapses in the next week, next day, and next hour, using features engineered from a 4X daily EMA. These immediate risk models can provide personalized support recommendations to address risks for possible lapses in that moment.

In Study 2, we evaluated and interpreted models that use the same 4× daily EMA features to predict alcohol lapses occurring within a 24-hour window up to two weeks into the future. These models with lagged prediction windows provide insight into lapse risk with the added benefit of advanced warning, giving individuals extra time to make behavioral, social, or lifestyle changes. 

In Study 3, we extended lapse prediction to opioid use using a national sample of participants with opioid use disorder. This allowed us to evaluate whether lapse prediction algorithms generalize beyond alcohol to substances where use is illegal and lapses may be underreported. We also supplemented EMA with geolocation to assess an alternative less budensome sensing method.

Across all three studies we evaluated the interpretability and fairness of model outputs.
:::



## Study 1

1. Evaluate and interpret models that predict <span style="color: #c5050c;">**immediate risk**</span> of a lapse back to alcohol use using 4x daily EMA

## Participants (*N* = 151)

```{r}
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-align: center
 
source("figs/dem_figs.R")

fig_aud
```


:::{.notes}
I am going to introduce the first dataset that we used for study 1 and study 2. 

Our sample consisted of 151 participants with moderate to severe alcohol use disorder living in the Madison, WI area. As a reminder, all of our participants had a goal of abstinence which made it easier for us to identify discrete lapse onsets.
:::


## Participants (*N* = 151)

```{r}
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-align: center

library(patchwork)

fig_race + fig_sex
```



:::{.notes}
Our sample was predominantly non-Hispanic White. We had equal representation of men and women.
:::

## Participants (*N* = 151)


```{r}
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-align: center

fig_age + fig_income
```

:::{.notes}
The average age was 41 years and average income was $34000.
:::


## Design

- 3-month longitudinal study
- Participants completed 4 surveys daily
    - Craving, arousal, valence 
    - Past stressful events, risky situations, pleasant events
    - Future stressful events, risky situations, efficacy
    - Alcohol use


::: {.notes}

Participants completed 4 daily surveys for up to three months

Each survey had 10 items that asked about 
The greatest intensity of craving, arousal, and valence since the last survey.  

Stressful events, risky situations and pleasant events encountered since their last survey

Future stressful events, risky situations and efficacy looking in the next week

And any alcohol use they had not yet reported.

We used all 10 EMA items plus some demographic questions to engineer our features. 
:::

## Design

- Outcome = lapse or no lapse
- XGBoost algorithm
- Grouped nested k-fold cross-validation
- True prediction into the future



::: {.notes}
There are a couple of additional methods details that are important to know and they apply to all three studies.
  
The start time and day of substance use reported on EMA was used to generate our outcome labels of lapse or no lapse. 

We considered several candidate statistical algorithms, but for all studies the best performing algorithm was XGBoost.  

We used grouped nested cross validation – this method produces INDEPENDENT, UNBIASED estimates of how well the model performs in NEW DATA
That means the data used for model evaluation was not used to train the models. Our data was also grouped by participant, so all of a participant’s data was either in training or test.

Lastly, all of our features used in the models were collected before the lapse onset. So when I say predict I mean we are predicting on new data from new individuals but we are also predicting into the future. 
:::


## EMA Completion

:::{.columns}
:::{.column width = "50%"}

```{r}
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-width: 7

source("figs/lag_figs.R")

fig_compliance
```

:::

:::{.column width = "50%"}
- 3.1 of 4 surveys completed daily (78.4% compliance)
- 95% compliance for completing at least one survey daily
:::
:::

:::{.notes}
Participants on average completed 3 of the 4 EMAs each day and completed at least one EMA on 95.0% of days. 

The plot on the left shows the percentage of EMA completion looking at the full 4x daily protocol (in red) and completion of at least 1 daily EMA (in grey) over the number of weeks on study. We found participants on average were able to sustain high rates of EMA completion over the full three months.
:::

## Immediate Models


![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/immediate.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 left=150 height=80%}


:::{.notes}
We fit three immediate models that differed in the width of the prediction window.
The prediction windows were 1 week, 1 day, and 1 hour (shown in yellow).

The vertical dashed line represents the prediction time point—when the model is making the prediction.

So moving from top to bottom, we’re asking: at this exact moment, will the person lapse in the next week, next day, or next hour?
:::


## Performance

```{r}
#| fig-align: center

source("figs/ema_figs.R")
source("figs/lag_figs.R")

perf_ema_empty
```


:::{.notes}
We used area under the roc curve (auROC) to evaluate model performance. auroc is the probability that a randomly selected positive case will be assigned a higher probability compared to a randomly selected negative case.  Scores can range from .5, Chance performance - 1, perfect performance.
:::

## Performance

```{r}
#| fig-align: center

perf_ema
```



:::{.notes}
here, from left to right, are the median aurocs and credible intervals for predicting lapses in the next week, day, and hour. Median auROCs ranged from .90, to .93. 

Our Hour model on the far right performed better than both the Day and Week model

And the Day model performed better than Week model

Overall these models are doing very well - auROCs above .90 are typically considered to be excellent performance.
:::

## Performance

```{r}
#| fig-align: center

perf_ema_red
```



:::{.notes}
So we’ve established that we can predict lapse at different levels of precision.

But lets briefly zoom back out to our broader goal—building a recovery monitoring and support system that communicates daily risk to individuals.

A weekly prediction window is likely too coarse to be actionable, while an hourly model would require delivering risk updates every hour, which is not practical in a real-world setting.

In contrast, the next day (or next 24-hour) prediction window strikes a balance between these two extremes. It allows us to provide timely, meaningful risk information without overwhelming the individual.

Moving forward, I am going to focus exclusively on models with 24-hour prediction windows.
:::


## Model Fairness (Next-day)


```{r}
#| fig-height: 5.85
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-align: center

fair_0lag_empty
```


:::{.notes}

We explicitly evaluated how fair our next day models performed for three important subgroups with known disparities in access to substance use treatment and substance use outcomes – race and ethnicity (Hispanic and/or not White vs. non-Hispanic White), income (below poverty vs. above poverty), and sex at birth (female vs. male).
:::

## Model Fairness (Next-day)


```{r}
#| fig-height: 5.85
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-align: center

fair_0lag_2
```


:::{.notes}

Our models performed  worse for participants who were not white and had an income below the federal poverty line. 

The largest contributing factor is likely the lack of diversity in our training data.

Even with our coarse dichotomous grouping of race we only had 20 participants in the not White group (compared to 131 in the White group). We also saw a similar pattern in our income groups (with 49 people in the below poverty group and 102 people in the above poverty group).

:::

## Model Fairness (Next-day)

```{r}
#| fig-height: 5.85
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-align: center

fair_0lag_all
```


:::{.notes}

We did however have equal representation of men and women, and we still saw differences in performance. This was a bit surprising. I am not sure exactly why this difference exists but one hypothesis is that it is related to the features themself. We chose our EMA items using domain expertise from decades of research on the factors that predict relapse. However, up until 1993 women were mostly excluded from substance use treatment research. As a result, it is possible that these constructs more accurately predict relapse risk for men than women. And of course it is possible that this also applies to other groups under-represented in the literature.
:::


## Feature Importance (Next-day)



:::{.notes}
We can use metrics of feature importance to understand which features are influencing our models predictions on average.

Since our best performing model was XGBoost we calculated Shapley values. Shapley values indicate how much a feature score for a specific observation adjusted the prediction relative to the mean prediction across all observations.

:::


## Feature Importance (Next-day)

```{r}
#| fig-align: left
#| fig-width: 7.3
#| fig-height: 5.75

global_0lag
```


:::{.notes}
We aggregated the shapley values by calculating the mean absolute shapley value across all observations to see how much each feature, on average, contributed to the next day model's predictions.

Past use was the most important feature. This is not surprising given that our outcome was lapse, and past behavior is often the best predictor of future behavior. 

Abstinence self-efficacy emerged as second most important, indicating that participants had reasonably accurate insight into their near-term abstinence success. Craving was also an important predictor that it may be an important target for intervention to support early recovery efforts.

As a reminder this approach summarizes overall feature importance by averaging the magnitude of each feature’s contribution. A down-side is that it can be skewed toward features that exhibit infrequent but very large Shapley values. So for example less than 8% of observations contained lapses and almost half of our participants didn't lapse at all on study. This means that past use may not actually represent what feature was most important on most days for most individuals.

So perhaps what we really want to know is how often these features came up as the most important feature on a given day for a given individual. 
:::

## Feature Importance (Next-day)

```{r}
#| fig-align: left
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5.85

global_0lag + global_0lag_v2
```

:::{.notes}
The plot on the right summarises the proportion of observations in which each feature had the highest Shapley value. So now considering the magnitude of the influence and the prevalance.

This plot shows that although past use was the most influential feature overall, it was only the most influential feature on a subset of days. 

In fact, past and future self-reported stressful events came up as the most important feature on the most days despite this having a small influence on predictions globally.

This shows that that while some features may have low average importance (across all individuals and observations), they can be highly important on certain days for certain individuals and are worthwhile to keep in the model for delivering feedback personalized to the person and the moment.

:::


## Conclusions

- We can predict probabilities of lapse at varying levels of temporal precision with excellent performance

:::{.fragment}
- We can characterize the relative importance of features contributing to these risk probabilities for specific individuals at specific moments in time
:::

:::{.fragment}
- A lack of diversity in the training data makes these models not yet ready for implementation
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Well-suited for personalized treatment recommendations that can be done immediately
:::


:::{.notes}

In study 1 we found that


We can predict probabilities of lapse at varying levels of temporal precision with excellent performance

and we can characterize the relative importance of features contributing to these risk probabilities for specific individuals at specific moments in time

these models are not yet clinically implementable. The most notable flaw being the unfairness of model performance for non-majority groups.

These models that predict and characterize immediate risk of lapse are well-suited for personalized treatment recommendations that can be done immediately, for example within an app

Earlier I gave the example of an urge surfing recommendation. This could also include recommending a guided relaxation video when someone is reporting recent stressful events or encouraging individuals to reflect on recent past successes or reasons for choosing abstinence when self-efficacy is low.

What's key here is that this assumes that the recommendations can be implemented immediately, but for the best clinical recommendation this might not be feasible. 

:::



## Study 2

1. Evaluate and interpret models that predict <span style="color: #c5050c;">**immediate risk**</span> of a lapse back to alcohol use using 4x daily EMA

2. Evaluate and interpret models that predict <span style="color: #c5050c;">**future risk**</span> of a lapse back to alcohol use using 4x daily EMA

:::{.notes}
So in study 2, we built additional models to predict future risk of lapses back to alcohol use. In these models we shifted a 24 hour prediction window 1 day, 3 days, 1 week or 2 weeks into the future. 

These “time-lagged” models could give people advanced warning to implement supports not immediately available to them, such as scheduling positive or pleasant activities, increasing social engagement, or attending a peer-led recovery meeting.
:::


## Lagged Predictions


![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/lag.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 left=150 height=80% style="z-index:1;"}




:::{.notes}

I just want to take a moment to be clear about what I mean when I say lagged predictions. So in study one I was manipulating the prediction window width. 

In this study my prediction window widths are all 24 hours (shown in yellow)

like before the vertical dashed line represents the prediction time point 

:::


## Lagged Predictions


![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/lag.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 left=150 height=80% style="z-index:1;"}


![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/arrow.png?raw=true){.absolute top=225 left=150 height=10% style="z-index:2;"}


:::{.notes}

This first row depicts the immediate model I showed you previously. At a specific time point what is the probability of a lapse in the next 24 hours

In the following rows I begin to lag this prediction window further away from the prediction timepoint with the grey shaded portion of the bar representing this lag time.

:::


## Lagged Predictions



![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/lag.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 left=150 height=80% style="z-index:1;"}


![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/arrow.png?raw=true){.absolute top=300 left=150 height=10% style="z-index:2;"}


:::{.notes}

So here the prediction window is lagged by one day. This means at a specific time point what is the probability of a lapse in a 24 hour window 1 day from now.

:::


## Lagged Predictions



![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/lag.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 left=150 height=80% style="z-index:1;"}

![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/arrow.png?raw=true){.absolute top=375 left=150 height=10% style="z-index:2;"}


:::{.notes}

what is the probability of a lapse three days from now

:::


## Lagged Predictions


![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/lag.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 left=150 height=80% style="z-index:1;"}

![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/arrow.png?raw=true){.absolute top=440 left=150 height=10% style="z-index:2;"}


:::{.notes}

1 week from now

:::



## Lagged Predictions

![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/lag.png?raw=true){.absolute top=100 left=150 height=80% style="z-index:1;"}

![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/arrow.png?raw=true){.absolute top=510 left=150 height=10% style="z-index:2;"}


:::{.notes}

and two weeks from now
:::



## Performance

```{r}
#| fig-align: center

perf_lag_empty
```

:::{.notes}

Next I will show the performance for our lagged models, anchored to our immediate (no lag) day level model from study 1 shown here in red

::::

## Performance

```{r}
#| fig-align: center

perf_lag
```

:::{.notes}
As models were lagged further into the future model performance decreased. However, even at our most lagged model, 2-weeks into the future the median auROC was .84 which is considered good performance.
:::


## Model Fairness (2-week lag)

```{r}
#| fig-height: 5.7
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-align: center

fair_336lag_all
```


:::{.notes}
Here I am showing the fairness analyses for our most lagged model (2 weeks). Given we are using the same data set it is not surprising that we saw the same fairness patterns in model performance. Though it appears these discrepancies in performance may even be worse when predicting further out into the future. 
:::

## Feature Importance (2-week lag)

```{r}
#| fig-align: left
#| fig-width: 8.1
#| fig-height: 5.75

global
```


:::{.notes}
Here are the average contributions of each feature to the model's predictions for our immediate model from study 1 in red and our two week lagged model in grey. So this is that traditional characterization of overall feature importance by mean absolute shapley value.

Several feature categories displayed sizeable differences in global importance by lag time. A couple interesting observations 

The importance of abstinence self-efficacy dropped by more than 50% in the 2-week lagged model relative to the immediate model. This may indicate that self-efficacy during early recovery is unstable even across shorter periods of time such that their current self-efficacy may predict immediate abstinence success but not necessarily success two weeks into the future. 

We also saw that past and anticipated risky situations were more important in the 2-week lagged model compared to the immediate model. This could suggest that the impact of these situations on lapses back to use may be delayed.
:::

## Feature Importance (2-week lag)

```{r}
#| fig-align: left
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5.85

global_no_legend + global_v2
```

:::{.notes}
This next plot shows the proportion of days each feature was the top feature by lag time. In red is the immediate no lag model so we see the same patterns as before there. With the two week lagged model in grey, we see that past and future risky situations remained to be a top feature. Additionally, valence and arousal emerged as a top feature on more days compared to the immediate model. I found this interesting because valence and arousal can fluctuate rapidly. But this could mean that changes in these states reflect a vulnerability to lapse that accumulates over time, rather than immediately triggering a lapse.
:::

## Conclusions

- We can achieve clinically meaningful performance up to 2 weeks into the future

:::{.fragment}
- These models offer the benefit of advanced notice for interventions not immediately available
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Important lapse risk factors may differ depending on the time scale of the prediction
:::

:::{.notes}
In study 2 we found that we can achieve clinically meaningful performance up to two weeks into the future. 

Model performance did decrease when prediction windows were shifted further into the future. Still, the benefit of advanced notice of lapse risk likely outweighs the cost to performance.

Important lapse risk factors may differ depending on the time scale of the prediction

:::



## Study 3

1. Evaluate and interpret models that predict <span style="color: #c5050c;">**immediate risk**</span> of a lapse back to alcohol use using 4x daily EMA

2. Evaluate and interpret models that predict <span style="color: #c5050c;">**future risk**</span> of a lapse back to alcohol use using 4x daily EMA

3. Evaluate and interpret models that predict <span style="color: #c5050c;">**immediate risk**</span> of a lapse back to opioid use using 1x daily EMA and geolocation sensing

:::{.notes}
Now for study three we are going to switch datasets. In this study we developed a machine learning model that predicts next-day opioid lapse risk using daily self-report and geolocation sensing data. 

A couple differences with this dataset aside from substance of use:

First, we recruited a national sample of participants across the United States in attempt to increase diversity of our participants.

Second, participants provided sensing data and opioid lapse reports for up to one year, a duration that exceeds previously published substance use prediction studies and allowed us to capture longer-term recovery trajectories.
:::

## Participants (*N* = 296)

```{r}
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-align: center
 
source("figs/risk2_dem_figs.R")

fig_oud
```


:::{.notes}
Our sample consisted of 296 participants in the US in early recovery from opioid use disorder. To be eligible, participants must have been enrolled in a medication for OUD program or recently completed a residential treatment program. The majority of our participants had severe opioid use disorder.
:::


## Participants (*N* = 296)

```{r}
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-align: center

fig_race + fig_sex
```



:::{.notes}
Our sample was predominantly White, but had some more representation of Black and Hispanic participants compared to our previous sample. We had slightly more men than women.
:::

## Participants (*N* = 296)


```{r}
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-align: center

fig_age + fig_income
```

:::{.notes}
Most participants were between 26-45 years and had an annual household income below $25,000
:::



## Design

- 12-month longitudinal study
- Participants completed 1 daily survey
    - Craving, pain, risky situations, stressful events, and pleasant events
    - Depressed, angry, anxious, relaxed, and happy
    - Sleep quality
    - MOUD adherence
    - Motivation and confidence
    - Opioid and other drug and alcohol use

    
:::{.notes}
Participants completed 1X daily surveys for up to 12 months

16 items -

intensity of craving, pain, risky situations, stressful events, and pleasant events since their last survey.

How depressed, angry, anxious, relaxed, and happy they have felt in the past 24 hours

Their quality of sleep

Whether they took their MOUD as prescribed. 

How motivated and how confident they were to avoid using opioids,

The dates and times of any previously unreported past opioid use and any other drugs or alcohol used in the past 24 hours 

:::



## Geolocation 

- Type of location
- Activity done at location
- Pleasantness/unpleasantness of experience
- Helpfulness/harmfulness to recovery

:::{.notes}
Each month we asked participants self-report questions about their frequently visited locations. We did this as part of a longer monthly survey participants were paid to take each month.

For places participants visited more than twice in a month we asked:
about the type of location, what they do there, how pleasant and unpleasant their experience is there, and how much this place helps and harms their recovery from opioids.

:::

## Adherence

:::{.columns}
:::{.column width = "50%"}

```{r}
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-width: 7

source("figs/risk2_figs.R")

fig_risk2_compliance
```

:::

:::{.column width = "50%"}
- Mean days on study = 278.32 days (range 30-392 days)

:::{}
- 1X daily EMA adherence = 77.8% (range 40-100%)
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Mean daily geolocation points = 263.50 points (range 2.17-871.34 points)
:::
:::
:::

:::{.notes}
Participants remained on study for an average of 278 days 

And on average participants completed 77.8% of the daily EMA prompts 

The plot on the left shows average ema completion rates by month on study

Participants provided an average of 263 daily geolocation points

I will note that we have a wide range here, however it is difficult to provide an adherence summary of geolocation. Sampling rates are impacted by how much a person is moving. Low points might mean someone is stationary, it might mean they purposefully turned their geolocation tracking off or it might mean their phone died, they have poor internet connection to transmit their geolocation data or other technological issues. 
:::



## Performance

```{r}
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 10

risk2_perf_1
```

:::{.notes}
Our model performed very well with an overall auROC  of 0.94. To evaluate the incremental predictive value of EMA and geolocation sensing features, we also evaluated performance of models that ablated each set of features individually and together.
:::

## Performance

```{r}
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 10

risk2_perf_all
```

:::{.notes}
Model performance was comparable when geolocation features were removed from the model. And performance dramatically fell when ema features were removed on their own or in combination with geolocation features. This suggests that geolocation data may not be adding any signal above and beyond what EMA is capturing. That being said Geolocation did capture some signal, in the ablated EMA model performance was still above chance at .63.
:::


## Model Fairness
```{r}
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-height: 5.85

risk2_fair
```

:::{.notes}
We evaluated our full model across five dichotomous subgroups -  gender, income, race and ethnicity, sexual orientation, and geographic location.

There was evidence that model performance differed for all group comparisons. Notably, our model performed better for individuals with an annual income of less than \$25,000 compared to individuals with an annual income greater than \$25,000, thus favoring the disadvantaged group. 

While differences in performance estimates exist, this model is likely still clinically meaningful across subgroups, as all subgroups yielded median auROCs between 0.91 - 0.95

And it certainly is much better than our models in studies 1-2
:::


## Feature Importance

```{r}
#| fig-align: left
#| fig-width: 7.5
#| fig-height: 6

global_risk2
```

:::{.notes}
Here are our 20 most important features. 


Feature importance revealed the most important feature was differences in past opioid use. These feature was nearly two-fold more important than the next most important feature, raw urge ratings. This plot displays the top 30 (out of 186) feature categories, as defined by the 30 largest absolute mean shapley values.

I also want to point out that although GPS may not be providing unique signal for prediction, some features still emerged as important features in the model. So it may be worthwhile still to have geolocation features in the model if we want more opportunities for intervention.


:::

## Feature Importance

```{r}
#| fig-align: left
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6

global_risk2 + global_risk2_v2
```

:::{.notes}


In this approach, the most important feature was raw urge ratings, followed by raw abstinence confidence ratings. Differences in past opioid use was the third most important feature by this method, suggesting that while it has a strong relationship with lapse risk, it is not as prevalent across observations as other features. Notably, in this approach several additional comparably important features emerged, including differences in past pleasant experiences, differences in past stimulant use, and differences in time spent in helpful locations.
:::




## Conclusions

- EMA is a feasible even when measured for up to a year

:::{.fragment}
- GPS does not offer any unique incremental predictive value beyond EMA
:::

:::{.fragment}
- It may still be worthwhile to include GPS features
:::

:::{.notes}
EMA remains to be a feasible and predictive signal even when measured over a year.

If the goal is prediction, GPS features are not necessary as they do not offer any unique incremental predictive value beyond EMA. 

However, if the goal is to have a more nuanced understanding of lapse risk across a more diverse array of risk features than GPS is helpful. Adding GPS features did not hurt model performance and several of them emerged in the top subset of features. 

These findings suggest that although GPS features may not provide unique signal for prediction, it may be worthwhile to still include them in the model to expand opportunities for intervention and provide precision-oriented recovery support.

:::

## Future Directions

- Expand risk features


:::{.notes}
In terms of where to go next. One consideration might be to extend the number of risk features we are measuring to increase our ability to personalize recommendations.

EMA was shown to be a robust sensing method for lapse prediction even when dropped to only 1 daily survey. Adding additional items could improve model performance among subgroups where performance disparities exist. For example, some research suggests interpersonal relationship problems and hormonal changes are more salient lapse risk factors for women compared to men. However, we can't add too many additional items without making our survey immensely more burdensome for people to complete.

:::


## Future Directions

- Expand risk features

- Improve GPS sensing


:::{.notes}
One way to expand risk features without increasing burden is to supplement self-report data with more passive sensing methods. We started to do this with GPS data in study 3. 

And while our results may appear somewhat discouraging for its predictive value it does much better than other passive methods I have explored like cellular communications. So I believe theres still a case to be made for using GPS data and we had some methodological limitations worth noting.

First Although geolocation data can, in principle, be collected continuously, in practice these data are difficult to collect reliably. In our study, geolocation features had substantially higher rates of missingness compared to daily surveys. 

Second, our feature engineering pipeline relied on known locations with self-reported context. Passive methods for contextualizing location could provide risk-relevant contextual information across the full range of locations participants visited. For example, latitude and longitude coordinates could be enriched using publicly available datasets (e.g., alcohol outlet density, area deprivation index, and Narcotics Anonymous meeting times and locations). 

As geolocation sensing methods become more robust and missingness is reduced, these features may become more powerful for lapse prediction.
:::

## Future Directions

- Expand risk features

- Improve GPS sensing

- Include individuals with non-abstinent recovery goals


:::{.notes}
Another area important to explore is how our models might generalize to individuals with non-abstinent recovery goals.

Our models were trained to predict goal-inconsistent use, which for all of our participants pursuing abstinence meant any alcohol use. The definition of goal-inconsistent use is less straightforward for individuals who do not aim to abstain and may vary considerably from person to person.

This may require considering alternative outcomes. For example, heavy drinking days—often defined as risky or hazardous use—may represent a more consistent marker of goal-inconsistent behavior across individuals in recovery.

Another option is to allow individuals to self-define goal-inconsistent use (e.g., drinking on a weekday, drinking more than planned) and to train models in samples with more diverse recovery goals.
:::


## Future Directions

- Expand risk features

- Improve GPS sensing

- Include individuals with non-abstinent recovery goals

![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/happy.png?raw=true){.absolute top=320 left=350 height="48%"}


:::{.notes}
More broadly, focusing exclusively on drinking or substance use as the primary outcome may overlook other clinically meaningful indicators of recovery. Outcomes such as well-being, mental health, functional status, and quality of life may be equally—if not more—important markers of progress.
:::




## Future Directions

- Optimize the delivery of risk-relevant feedback



:::{.fragment}
![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/bubble_1.png?raw=true){.absolute left=100 width=22%}
:::

:::{.fragment}
![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/bubble_2.png?raw=true){.absolute left=250 bottom=10 width=35%}
:::

:::{.fragment}
![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/bubble_3.png?raw=true){.absolute right=330 width=30%}
:::

:::{.fragment}
![](https://github.com/KendraPaquette/presentations/blob/main/images/bubble_4.png?raw=true){.absolute right=40 bottom=10 width=30%}
:::

::: {.notes}

Returning again to the idea that these models will be most effective embedded in a recovery monitoring and support system.  

One crucial step is to investigate how to communicate information from our model output to individuals. Our models give us predicted probabilities of a lapse. One option would be to create thresholds for what constitutes low, medium, or high risk and relay current and future risk information to the individual. 

Another option would be to anchor the predicted probability to the individual. For example, is their risk higher or lower compared to the previous week.

But another thing our model's give us are the most important features driving that lapse prediction. We could imagine contextualizing lapse risk by alerting individuals to the most important predictor of their risk or using domain expertise to provide specific recommendations for recovery engagement.

In a currently funded grant in our lab, we plan to look what combination of risk-relevant information lead to optimal engagement and clinical recovery outcomes. 



A challenge for embedding lapse prediction models into a recovery monitoring and support system is determining how to effectively communicate model outputs to individuals. Our models generate quantitative predictions of lapse probability. These probabilities could be converted into categorical classifications (e.g., lapse vs. no lapse) by applying a decision threshold. Although we observed high sensitivity and specificity when thresholds were selected to balance these metrics, the positive predictive value (PPV) of categorical predictions was moderate to low in our full models. As a result, we believe categorical predictions should be communicated to patients with extreme caution, if at all. Instead, we favor the use of quantitative lapse probabilities as continuous risk indicators to guide supportive interventions and monitoring intensity.

However, raw probability estimates present a different problem. They may be difficult for patients, especially for those with low numeracy, to interpret intuitively (e.g., distinguishing the practical meaning of a 79% versus a 64% lapse probability) [@garcia-retameroCommunicatingHealthRisks2013]. Anchoring risk estimates to an individual’s personal baseline may enhance interpretability. For example, by emphasizing whether risk is higher or lower than in the prior week or relative to their typical pattern. Additionally, combining quantitative scores with coarser qualitative descriptors (e.g., low, moderate, high risk) may further support comprehension without oversimplification.

Beyond risk probabilities, our models also identify the most influential features contributing to each prediction. These locally important features create opportunities to contextualize risk feedback by highlighting key drivers of vulnerability (e.g., craving, stress, social isolation) and pairing them with domain-informed recovery recommendations. Emerging best practices from interpretable machine learning suggest this information may make the machine learning model less of a "black box", which may build trust, or allow individuals to engage more thoughtfully (e.g., questioning false positives, scrutinizing model behavior) and gain insight [@dacruzUsingInterpretabilityApproaches2021; @shinEffectsExplainabilityCausability2021].

Despite these opportunities, there is currently no consensus on how best to present predictive feedback in a manner that maximizes engagement, trust, and clinical utility. Prior research suggests that increasing transparency in machine learning systems, such as by revealing contributing factors, can improve perceived credibility and user acceptance [@kizilcecHowMuchInformation2016; @kuleszaPrinciplesExplanatoryDebugging2015; @limWhyWhyNot2009]. In the context of recovery monitoring, selective transparency may also promote self-awareness and learning, potentially yielding additional clinical benefits [@kuleszaPrinciplesExplanatoryDebugging2015; @kuleszaFixingProgramMy2009]. At the same time, overly detailed or complex explanations may undermine confidence by exposing model uncertainty, revealing occasional errors, or burdening users with unnecessary information [@buntAreExplanationsAlways2012; @springerProgressiveDisclosureWhen2020]. Thus, determining the optimal balance between transparency and simplicity remains an open empirical question.

As immediate next steps, we have begun to address the gap in understanding how to deliver risk‑related feedback within a recovery monitoring and support system. We have recently launched an NIAAA‑funded study focused on optimizing daily support messages delivered by such a system [@wyantMaximizingEngagementTrust2025]. This work will explicitly examine which combinations of key message components, including lapse probability, important features, and recovery recommendations, maximize engagement, trust, and clinical outcomes.

In this grant, messages are generated by feeding model outputs into a large language model to produce daily messages with sufficient variation. In the near future, however, these model outputs may be integrated into a more fully autonomous, agentic support system capable of dynamically delivering personalized recommendations, engaging individuals in interactive problem‑solving, facilitating connections to therapists or peer support specialists, and initiating triage processes when risk reaches clinically concerning levels.
:::




## Thank You! 


:::{.notes}
John for letting me lead these exciting analyses for his grants, mentorship
Lab 
Cohort
Family
:::
